title: Artemisia minutiflora
taxonomy: Artemisia minutiflora is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This classification places it alongside other plants with similar characteristics, such as dandelions and thistles. The genus Artemisia is a group of flowering plants that includes sagebrush and wormwood. These plants are typically found in temperate regions and are known for their distinctive aromas. Within the family Asteraceae, Artemisia minutiflora is further classified within the tribe Anthemideae.

intro: Artemisia minutiflora, also known as small-flowered wormwood, is a perennial herb belonging to the Asteraceae family, characterized by its small, yellow or white flowers and bitter, aromatic foliage. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties, which are said to aid in the treatment of various ailments, such as digestive issues and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Artemisia minutiflora include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and essential oils, including thujone, which is responsible for its bitter flavor and medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the plant is commonly prepared as a tea, tincture, or infusion, although its use is not without controversy due to the presence of thujone, which can be toxic in large quantities.
